content.text: are strategies for maximizing Claude's 1M token window effectiveness while avoiding performance degradation, cost burn, and the "lost-in-the-middle" problem. Even with 1M context,
— you need to strategically organize content to hit the model's 30–40% "prime time" accuracy zone.
careful with context management, not less.
: Model recall drops even though context "fits"
: 1M context becomes noise for agentic tasks
Don't rely on washing machine behavior

: Combined with Memory Tool + Context Editing
-
Use git worktrees for separate agents

0–300K tokens:  ✅ Excellent recall (start = prime zone)
300K–700K tokens: ⚠️ Gradual degradation (middle = lost-in-the-middle)
700K–1M tokens:  ⚠️ Significant degradation (end = recovery zone)

1M context → compaction still triggers at ~95% (950K)
Compaction = "washing machine"
  ├─ Takes entire context → spin-dries into summary
  └─ Result: 50K tokens but loses critical details

200K context:  ~167K usable tokens (83% efficiency)
1M context:    ~830K usable tokens (83% efficiency)
: Start with 200K, move to 1M when ready
: Formulate comprehensive plan → clear context → execute
: Clear before embarking on substantial task
: Draft with Opus, execute with Sonnet
: Separate agents to maintain streamlined context
: Collaborate via written docs, not SendMessage
: Place critical info at start/end, not middle.

# Workflow:
1. Develop comprehensive plan (OpenSpec or built-in planning)
2. /clear to reset everything (like Claude's planning mode)
3. Execute plan with full context
4. Make adjustments while model stays focused
5. Repeat process
: It retains remnants from previous interactions.
: Opus makes fewer mistakes on long context, but Sonnet is 1.7x cheaper.
Use Batch API for 50% Cost Savings

# Separate agents with git worktrees
git worktree add /project/agent-1 auth-feature
git worktree add /project/agent-2 payment-feature
git worktree add /project/agent-3 order-feature

# Each agent operates with its own worktree
# Prevents overlap between agents' files
: You can throw more in, but don't burn through full million.

# ❌ Wrong: /compact retains previous context remnants
/compact "Keep only auth refactor plan"

# ✅ Right: /clear removes everything, then start fresh
/clear
# Paste new plan
: Model performs more efficiently when not managing outdated context.

# Step 1: Plan mode
"Analyze auth/ module and propose refactoring plan to replace callbacks with async/await"

# Step 2: Clear context
/clear

# Step 3: Execute with fresh context
"Implement plan:\n[plan content]"

# Step 4: Adjust while model stays focused
"Adjust: Add refresh token rotation to step 3"
: 15% fewer compaction events, full context maintained.
: Avoids excessive token consumption from constant updates.
: Agent uses fewer total tokens, more efficient.

Large diffs didn't fit → agent splits context → loses cross-file dependencies
→ Can't see how change in file A breaks interface in file B
: Single pass, see how change in one file breaks interface in another.
Process 100 pages → chunk → summarize → lose critical details
: Process 600 pages at once, analyze all 5 versions in one session.
: What was once impossible is now reality — full codebase + docs + tests, all at once.

: "What was once impossible is now reality"
-
: Work on 75,000+ lines while maintaining high accuracy

: Raised window from 200K to 500K → agent became more efficient
-
: Agent uses fewer tokens despite larger window
Draft → implement → new plan for adjustments
Draft with Opus, execute with Sonnet
: ZDR eligible → data not stored after API response
: Cap at 600 images/PDF pages per request
Draft with Opus, execute with Sonnet
For your Box Hill distributed systems work, I'd implement
/clear + plan mode + Opus draft/Sonnet execute + 30–40% prime time organization
. This gives you 15% fewer compactions, 50% cost savings, and 78.3% recall accuracy while avoiding the "more context isn't better" trap.
